samples.label: pricing decision under ownership ambiguity
samples.inputSummary: ownership ambiguity between sales and finance the enterprise pricing change Q3 enterprise renewal revenue Who owns this decision? Unclear — pricing sits between sales and finance What evidence do you have? Churn analysis from March and two enterprise renewal objections What happens if you wait? Competitor undercut continues through Q3
samples.outputText:
The dominant decision friction in your situation is ownership ambiguity between sales and finance, surfaced directly by your answers about the enterprise pricing change.
Taken together, your answers suggest the decision is stalling on ownership ambiguity between sales and finance rather than on missing information: the pattern across your responses points at the enterprise pricing change as the live pressure point.
If this friction is left unaddressed, the stake you named — Q3 enterprise renewal revenue — degrades by default: the decision gets made late, reactively, or by whoever feels the pressure first instead of whoever owns it.
The minimum viable correction is to name a single decision owner for the enterprise pricing change, write the decision in one sentence, and set a 48-hour checkpoint to confirm whether the friction has moved.
This result reads the friction pattern in your answers only. It does not yet prove root cause, the size of the commercial exposure, or that the correction will hold under organisational pressure — that requires fuller evidence than a fast diagnostic collects.
Escalate beyond this result when the decision involves irreversible spend, more than one accountable owner, customer or regulatory exposure, or when the 48-hour checkpoint shows the friction has not moved.
Within the next 10 minutes: write the one-sentence decision statement for the enterprise pricing change, name its owner, and put the 48-hour checkpoint in the owner's calendar. That single act converts this diagnosis into a governed decision.

samples.label: post-merger operations team with conflicting priorities
samples.inputSummary: three of five team leads gave materially conflicting answers about this quarter's top priority the team assessment responses a twelve-person operations team three months after a merger an alignment illusion — agreement in meetings, divergence in execution
samples.outputText:
Signal: three of five team leads gave materially conflicting answers about this quarter's top priority, observed in the team assessment responses against your situation — a twelve-person operations team three months after a merger.
What this points at: an alignment illusion — agreement in meetings, divergence in execution. The signal matters because it appears where your situation and the evidence intersect, not as a general industry observation.
Practical next action: test the signal against your own case this week — take the single decision it touches, write down who owns it and what evidence would confirm or kill the signal, and review that note within seven days.
Honest limitation: this signal is drawn from the team assessment responses and your stated situation only. It does not prove causation, scale, or that your case matches the underlying pattern — it earns attention, not conviction.
Escalation condition: if the signal touches an irreversible commitment, live customer exposure, or a decision already under deadline pressure, move it from observation into a governed review before acting on it.
